perturbation.original_text: If a legal action between the Parties arises from this Agreement or the conduct of any Party with respect to any Confidential Information, the prevailing Party shall recover from the other Party its reasonable attorney fees and costs. No waiver of any right under, or breach of, this Agreement shall be effective unless in writing and si gned by an authorized representative of the party against whom the waiver is sought to be enforced.
perturbation.changed_text: No waiver of any right under, or breach of, this Agreement shall be effective unless in writing and si gned by an authorized representative of the party against whom the waiver is sought to be enforced. If a legal action between the Parties arises from this Agreement or the conduct of any Party with respect to any Confidential Information, the prevailing Party shall recover from the other Party its reasonable attorney fees and costs.
perturbation.explanation: By moving the attorney fees clause to the end of the section, one could argue that its application is limited to the waiver portion, suggesting attorney fees only apply if the dispute concerns a waiver of rights, not any general breach. This may reduce the likelihood of a party seeking legal action, especially for smaller violations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The principle of ejusdem generis suggests that where general words follow an enumeration of specific items, the general words are construed to embrace only objects similar in nature to those enumerated by the specific words. By repositioning the attorney fees clause immediately after the waiver clause, one could argue that 'legal action' is limited in scope to actions specifically related to waivers, rather than any and all legal actions arising from the agreement, undermining its broad applicability and chilling enforcement of other breaches.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ejusdem generis | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you ejusdem generis Ejusdem generis (ee-joose-dem gen-ris) is a Latin phrase that means “of the same kind.” The statutory and constitutional construction principle of “ejusdem generis” states that where general words or phrases follow a number of specific words or phrases, the general words are specifically construed as limited and apply only to persons or things of the same kind or class as those expressly mentioned. For example, if a law refers to automobiles, trucks, tractors, motorcycles, and other motor-powered vehicles, a court might use ejusdem generis to hold that such vehicles would not include airplanes, because the list included only land-based transportation. Ejusdem generis is an interpretive guide for a contract under New York law. The rule is used only to help determine whether there is intent ; if intent is found, ejusdem generis does not subvert intent. [Last reviewed in February of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords statutory interpretation Wex THE LEGAL PROCESS wex definitions legal theory Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts legal principles by potentially limiting the scope of the attorney's fees clause. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both highlight how moving the attorney fees clause could be interpreted under ejusdem generis to apply only to disputes related to waiver, not to broader breaches of the agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines ejusdem generis as a principle where 'general words are specifically construed as limited and apply only to persons or things of the same kind or class as those expressly mentioned.' By placing the attorney's fees clause immediately after the waiver clause, one could argue that 'legal action' is limited to actions related to waivers. This weakens the enforceability of the contract regarding other breaches, creating a structural flaw that introduces ambiguity about the intended scope of the attorney's fees clause, leading to a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Other than this Agreement, no contract or agreement providing for any relationshi p involving the Parties (whether relating to the Purpose or otherwise) will be deemed to exist between the Parties unless and until a final definitive agreement(s) has been executed and delivered by each of the Parties, and unless and until any such defini tive agreement(s) has been executed an delivered, neither Party will be under any legal obligation of any kind or nature whatsoever, except for the terms and conditions set forth in this Agreement
perturbation.changed_text: Other than this Agreement, no contract or agreement providing for any relationshi p involving the Parties (whether relating to the Purpose or otherwise) will be deemed to exist between the Parties unless and until a final definitive agreement(s) has been executed and delivered by each of the Parties, except for the terms and conditions set forth in this Agreement, and unless and until any such defini tive agreement(s) has been executed an delivered, neither Party will be under any legal obligation of any kind or nature whatsoever.
perturbation.explanation: By moving the 'except for the terms and conditions set forth in this Agreement' phrase to the middle of the sentence, before the second 'unless and until' clause, we create ambiguity as to whether the existing agreement's terms only apply if a final agreement isn't reached. This impacts enforceability by creating uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 206 (Contra Proferentem). When a contract clause is ambiguous, the ambiguity is resolved against the drafter of the agreement. By embedding the exceptions to the existence of legal obligation mid-sentence, the ambiguity created would allow a party to argue that the original agreement only applies if a later formal agreement isn't concluded, thus reducing the enforceability of the current agreement's confidentiality provisions. If the language were unclear about the agreement's continuation/survival beyond a failure to agree on final terms, a court could interpret it against the drafting party, reducing the contract's effectiveness.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contra proferentem | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contra proferentem Contra proferentem is a rule of contract interpretation that states an ambiguous contract term should be construed against the drafter of the contract. The term contra proferentem is derived from a Latin phrase meaning “ against the offeror .” Contra proferentem has become increasingly important with the rise of contracts of adhesion . Contracts of adhesion involve pre-written contracts which are offered on a strict take-it or leave-it basis, leaving no opportunity for a party to bargain over specific contractual terms. Because the party does not have an opportunity to negotiate, they may reasonably interpret a term in a different manner than the contract offeror intended. Contra proferentem exists to place the burden of ambiguity on the party most capable of mitigating that ambiguity - the person who wrote it. The doctrine of contra proferentem is also especially important in the field of insurance law due to the generalized nature of many of its terms. For example, it may be unclear if a policy that covers “water damage” will cover damage caused by a rainstorm induced mudslide. The doctrine of contra proferentem encouraged insurance providers to create enumerated lists of events that are excluded under a given policy, ultimately increasing clarity for insurance purchasers. perturbation.justification: The altered text introduces ambiguity regarding whether the terms of the initial agreement remain enforceable if a final agreement isn't reached. This contradicts the principle of contra proferentem, as outlined in the provided law snippet. The snippet states that an ambiguous contract term should be construed against the drafter of the contract, and the changed text creates ambiguity by repositioning the exception clause. This could allow a party to argue that the initial agreement only applies if a subsequent agreement isn't finalized, thus weakening its enforceability. The structural change directly leads to this ambiguity, fitting the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition.
